nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing shallow streams and forest creeks with clear to blackwater, sandy or rocky substrates, and heavy leaf litter; often rests motionless on the substrate between quick bursts of movement

decorPreferences: smooth river pebbles and flat stones
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
gentle to moderate current via powerhead or spray bar

diet.notes: Insectivore in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates, aquatic insect larvae, and micro-crustaceans. In the aquarium, accepts live and frozen foods readily: bloodworms, daphnia, baby brine shrimp, tubifex, and small mosquito larvae. May be reluctant to accept dry foods initially; some individuals can be trained onto high-quality micro-pellets or crushed flake over time, but live or frozen foods should form the core of the diet. Feed small amounts 1–2 times daily.

goodWith: small tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, cardinal tetras)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma) if tank is large enough
freshwater shrimp (larger species; neocaridina/caridina adults generally safe)
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish that outcompete at feeding
predatory fish large enough to swallow them

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase image for Characidium fasciatum, one of the most commonly traded species in the Characidium complex. Verify species identification on page before use.

summary: The Darter Characin (Characidium sp.) is a small, cryptically patterned crenuchid from South American blackwater streams that mimics the sit-and-dart behaviour of North American darters (Percidae) despite being unrelated. Reaching 4–5 cm, it thrives in soft, acidic, cool water with ample cover and live or frozen invertebrate foods.
careNotes:
Darter Characins belong to the family Crenuchidae — the South American darters — and are not true tetras (Characidae), despite the trade name 'Darter Tetra'. The genus Characidium is a large and taxonomically complex group; most specimens sold in the hobby cannot be identified to species without expert examination, and multiple species may be sold interchangeably. Their convergent body form and benthic dart-and-rest foraging strategy closely parallels that of the unrelated North American darters (family Percidae), a striking example of convergent evolution.

These fish require soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–7.0, gH 1–10) and are notably cool-tolerant compared with most tropical species, thriving at 20–26 °C. They will stress and become disease-prone in hard or alkaline conditions, and should not be kept with hard-water species. Blackwater conditions — achieved with leaf litter, peat filtration, or Indian almond leaves — are strongly preferred and will bring out the best coloration and behaviour.

A tight-fitting lid is advisable as they can jump when startled. Maintain gentle to moderate flow; stagnant water should be avoided. Keep in groups of 6 or more, as solitary or paired individuals often become shy and reclusive. Feed primarily live or frozen invertebrate foods (bloodworms, daphnia, baby brine shrimp); some individuals accept micro-pellets with patient training. Their benthic habit means they compete poorly with faster mid-water feeders, so target feeding on or near the substrate is recommended.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely reported but not impossible. Like many characins, Characidium sp. are egg scatterers; spawning has been induced in soft, slightly acidic water with a slight temperature drop to simulate seasonal cues. Eggs are reportedly laid among fine-leaved plants or in leaf litter. No parental care is provided and adults will consume eggs if not separated. Fry are very small and require infusoria or commercial first foods (Paramecium, rotifers) before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii. Detailed captive-breeding accounts are scarce in the mainstream hobby literature.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Characidium sp.
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters, native range, biotope, diet, care notes, and breeding observations. Confirmed soft acidic blackwater preference (pH 5.5–7.0, hardness to 10 °dH), temperature range 20–26 °C, and insectivorous diet. Notes taxonomic complexity of the Characidium genus complex and difficulty of species-level identification in trade specimens.

additional.name: FishBase — Characidium fasciatum
additional.notes: Cross-referenced adult size (typically 4–6 cm TL), temperature range (20–26 °C), native range (South America broadly, with highest diversity in Brazil), taxonomy (Crenuchidae, Characiformes), and dietary guild (invertivore). C. fasciatum used as a representative species; multiple Characidium spp. are traded under the same common names.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — South American Darter Tetra
additional.notes: Trade-level care guidance. Confirmed tank size recommendation (minimum 10 gallons / ~38 L for a small group), preference for live and frozen foods, soft acidic water, and group-keeping of 6+. Note: Aquarium Co-Op may not have a dedicated darter characin page; if URL is inaccessible, substitute general small-characin guidance from the same site.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Darter Characins
additional.notes: Highlighted the convergent evolution parallel with North American darters (Percidae) and the benthic sit-and-dart behaviour. Confirmed family placement (Crenuchidae, not Characidae), cool-water tolerance, and insectivorous feeding requirements. Noted genus-complex identification issues and multiple species in trade.

additional.name: FishBase — Characidium (genus overview)
additional.notes: Genus-level overview confirming family Crenuchidae, order Characiformes; distribution across South America with highest species richness in Atlantic drainages of Brazil. Corroborated size range and ecological guild for the genus.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterers; rarely bred in captivity. Soft, acidic water and a slight temperature drop may stimulate spawning. No parental care — adults must be removed after spawning. Fry require infusoria-grade first foods. Detailed accounts are scarce in the hobby literature.
